coredata.dc:title: Orange carbon dots as fluorometric/spectrophotometric-bimodal sensors for cerium (Ⅳ) and dopamine detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Yifan
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Convenient and accurate detection of cerium (Ⅳ) and dopamine (DA) in environment and biosystem is highly needed. In this work, orange emissive carbon dots (CDs) with excellent photostability and good biocompatibility were prepared. Upon the addition of Ce4+, the fluorescence (FL) of CDs was effectively quenched, while the absorption at 256 nm was significantly enhanced. Thus, CDs could act as fluorometric/spectrophotometric dual-responsive probes for the determination of Ce4+, with low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM. Besides, in the presence of DA, the FL of CDs was recovered, and the absorption at 288 nm was strengthened. As a result, the mixture of CDs + Ce4+ could detect DA via fluorometric/spectrophotometric dual mode, and the LOD was 0.85 μM. Benefiting from the advantages of high selectivity, fast response, and reliability, CDs were applied for tracing Ce4+ and DA in water, human urine and cells. Furthermore, we successfully built CDs-based paper sensor for the detection of Ce4+ and DA. This dual-readout strategy holds huge potential in biosensing, disease diagnosis, and environmental monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorometric
Spectrophotometric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Ce4+ and DA in real samples
CDs for detection of Ce4+ and DA
Fluorescence “ON-OFF-ON” strategy to detect Ce4+ and DA
Spectrophotometric detection of Ce4+ and DA
The mechanisms of detection Ce4+ and DA
Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of CDs
Cds-based paper sensor for detecting Ce4+ and DA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of CDs. The inset is the size distribution of CDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum. (c) Full XPS survey spectrum of CDs. HR XPS spectra of (d) C1s, (e) N1s and (f) O1s of CDs. (g) UV–vis absorption spectrum. The inset is optical images of CDs solution under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right). (h) Excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (i) The emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelength.

(a) Selective detection of Ce4+ by CDs. (b) Plot of the change in FL intensity of CDs (200 μg/mL) after the addition of Ce4+ (100 μM) over time. (c) FL spectra of CDs (200 μg/mL) with different concentration of Ce4+ (0–100 μM). (d) I0/I-1 of CDs with different Ce4+ concentration. (e) Linear relationship between I0/I-1 and Ce4+ concentration of (10–70 μM). (f) Selectivity (purple column) and competitive (yellow column) detection for Ce4+. (g) Selectivity detection for DA. (h) Plot of FL intensity of CDs + Ce4+ after DA addition versus time. (i) FL spectra of CDs in the presence of different DA concentration (0–70 μM). (j) I/I0-1 of CDs at different DA concentration. (k) Linear relationship between CDs and different concentration of DA (20–50 μM). (l) Selectivity (purple column) and competitive (yellow column) detection for DA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, Ce4+ and CDs + Ce4+. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs + Ce4+. (c) Line relationship between (A0-A)/A and the concentration of Ce4+. (d) UV–vis absorption spectrum of DA and CDs + Ce4++DA. (e) The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs + Ce4++DA. (f) Linear relationship between (A-A0)/A and the concentration of DA.

(a) The viability of L929 cells treated with CDs (0–125 μg/mL) for 24 h. FL imaging of L929 cells treated with (b) CDs, (c–e) CDs + Ce4+ and (f–g) CDs + Ce4++DA. (h) The images of CDs coated cellulose paper treated with Ce4+ and (i) the images of CDs+Ce4+ coated cellulose paper treated with DA. The images were taken under a 365 nm UV light.

(a) Synthesis of CDs for Ce4+ and DA detection. Applications of CDs for the determination of Ce4+ and DA in (b) tap water, drinking water and human urine, (c) live cells, (d) CDs-based paper sensor for sensing Ce4+ and DA.

head.ce:title: Orange carbon dots as fluorometric/spectrophotometric-bimodal sensors for cerium (Ⅳ) and dopamine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Convenient and accurate detection of cerium (Ⅳ) and dopamine (DA) in environment and biosystem is highly needed. In this work, orange emissive carbon dots (CDs) with excellent photostability and good biocompatibility were prepared. Upon the addition of Ce4+, the fluorescence (FL) of CDs was effectively quenched, while the absorption at 256 nm was significantly enhanced. Thus, CDs could act as fluorometric/spectrophotometric dual-responsive probes for the determination of Ce4+, with low limit of detection (LOD) of 0.14 μM. Besides, in the presence of DA, the FL of CDs was recovered, and the absorption at 288 nm was strengthened. As a result, the mixture of CDs + Ce4+ could detect DA via fluorometric/spectrophotometric dual mode, and the LOD was 0.85 μM. Benefiting from the advantages of high selectivity, fast response, and reliability, CDs were applied for tracing Ce4+ and DA in water, human urine and cells. Furthermore, we successfully built CDs-based paper sensor for the detection of Ce4+ and DA. This dual-readout strategy holds huge potential in biosensing, disease diagnosis, and environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Cerium is one of the most abundant lanthanides in the Earth's crust [1,2], which is widely used in metallurgical industry, agriculture, biomedicine, and other fields [3–6]. Its main ion Ce4+, has strong oxidation capacity, and can bind to the proteins, DNA, RNA and fatty acids in biological system [7,8]. A small amount of Ce4+ has antibacterial function [9], but its accumulation can interfere with gene expression and transcription, and damage organelles [10]. Excessive Ce4+ in the living organisms will lead to lung and liver thrombosis, and may also induce leukemia, acute myocardial infarction, and skin lesions [11–13]. On the other hand, dopamine (DA) is a hormone and neurotransmitter with multiple physiological functions in the regulation of the central nervous system [14,15]. Dysregulation of the DA system may contribute to the development of Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, Tourette syndrome, attention deficit hyperactivity syndrome and pituitary tumors [16,17]. As a consequence, the establishment of efficient and reliable technologies to selectively detect Ce4+ and DA has practical significance for environmental and biological applications.

Up to date, diverse techniques have been developed for Ce4+ and DA determination such as electrochemical assay [18,19], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [20], and high performance liquid chromatography [21]. However, most of them are costly, inconvenient, and time-consuming, which seriously restrict their further applications. By contrast, with the merits of simple instrumentation, high sensitivity, fast detection, and noninvasiveness, optical assays have been validated as efficient methods for Ce4+ and DA detection [15,22–24]. For example, E. Priyadarshinia et al. prepared GNPs to detect Ce4+ by colorimetric detection [23]. Huang et al. developed Pdots@AMP-Cu composite material to detect dopamine via the fluorescence method, which the LOD was 4 μM [15]. Nevertheless, a single detection mode is insufficient to ensure the detection accuracy. To improve the accuracy and reliability of results, exploiting multimodal sensors that can facilitate mutual correction by different signals on the same analyte is highly desired.

Carbon dots (CDs) are mono-dispersed, quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles, with a size of less than 10 nm [25,26], which have the outstanding features of excellent optical properties, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, low cost, and good biocompatibility [27,28]. These advantages endow CDs with wide applications in a variety of areas such as medical imaging [29–33], sensing [34,35], catalyst [36], antibiosis [37], and cancer theranostics [38–40]. Especially, CDs have been recognized as a popular sensing platform for the surveillance of metal ions [41,42], bioactive substances [43,44], and organic contaminants [45]. For example, Wang et al. developed green-emitting CDs to detect GSH in fungal cell [43]. Xu et al. prepared CDs to distinguish cancer cells from normal cells [46]. However, the majority of CDs at present are restricted to blue or green emission, CDs with long wavelength luminescence are relatively scarce.

In this work, we prepared orange emissive CDs via a solvothermal method with 1,2,4,5-benzenetetraamine tetrahydrochloride (BTT), L-glutamine and polyethylene glycol (PEG1500) as precursors. CDs emit orange fluorescence (FL) at 597 nm under 400 nm excitation (Scheme 1 ), CDs could serve as fluorometric/spectrophotometric dual-modal probes for selectively sensing Ce4+ with low LOD of 0.14 μM. Afterwards, the CDs + Ce4+ mixture could detect DA via fluorometric and spectrophotometric bimode, and LOD was 0.85 μM. For Ce4+, the detection limit (0.14 μM) is 5-fold lower than WHO's drinking water standard (0.7 μM). For DA, the 0.85 μM detection limit covers both normal (0.01–1 μM) and pathological urinary concentrations. The linear ranges satisfy all regulatory and clinical requirements. In addition, CDs provided a visual sensing platform for the determination of Ce4+ and DA in real samples and living cells. Furthermore, we successfully built CDs-based paper sensor for the detection of Ce4+ and DA.

CDs were prepared by two-steps solvothermal method (Scheme 1a). Briefly, BTT (47 mg) and L-Glutamine (24 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of DMF. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooled to room temperature, 200 mg of PEG1500 was added, and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. The resulting dark brown solution was dialyzed for 10 h with a dialysis bag (MWCO = 500) and finally freeze-dried to obtain CDs.

To detect Ce4+, 1.5 mL of Ce4+ (0–200 μM) solution was added to 1.5 mL of CDs (400 μg/mL) aqueous solution to prepare 3 mL of the test solution. The FL and UV–vis absorption spectra of the solution were recorded. To examine the selectivity of CDs for Ce4+, Ce4+ was replaced with other substances (Cr3+, CA, Glu, Nd3+, Pb2+, Urea, Ni2+, Co2+, L-Trp, Ce3+, NH4 +, L-Leu, Y3+, Mn2+, MEL) at a concentration of 100 μM under the same conditions. In order to study the competitiveness of other substances against the CDs detection of Ce4+, we added the substances (Cr3+, CA, Glu, Nd3+, Pb2+, Urea, Ni2+, Co2+, L-Trp, Ce3+, NH4 +, L-Leu, Y3+, Mn2+, MEL) at a concentration of 100 μM to the CDs + Ce4+ aqueous solution. All experiments were repeated three times at room temperature.

For the detection of DA, 1 mL of different concentration of DA (0–210 μM) solution was added to the mixture of Ce4+ (300 μM, 1 mL) and CDs (600 μg/mL, 1 mL) solution to form 3 mL of the test solution. The FL and UV–vis absorption spectra of the solution were recorded. To examine the selectivity of CDs + Ce4+ for DA, DA was replaced with other substances (Cr3+, Glu, Pro, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Ni2+, Urea, Y3+, Mn2+, L-Leu, Phe, Ser, L-Trp, Nd3+) at a concentration of 100 μM under the same conditions. In order to study the competitiveness of other substances against the CDs detection of Ce4+, we added the substances (Cr3+, Glu, Pro, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Ni2+, Urea, Y3+, Mn2+, L-Leu, Phe, Ser, L-Trp, Nd3+) at a concentration of 100 μM to the CDs + Ce4++DA aqueous solution. All experiments were repeated three times at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ce4+ and DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the actual performance of CDs for detecting Ce4+ and DA in real samples, we collected tap water and drinking water form Changchun University of Technology. Water samples were filtered through filter membrane before use. Artificial urine was purchased from Zhonglian Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Based on the linear relationship between the FL intensity of CDs and the Ce4+ concentration, the Ce4+ concentration in the samples was determined, and the recovery rate was obtained. The detection of DA was conducted in a similar way.

The CDs-based paper sensor was prepared using filter paper and CDs solution. The filter paper was completely immersed in 300 μg/mL of CDs solution for 10 min, and air dried naturally. Then, the FL change of CDs-loaded filter paper was detected under 365 nm UV lamp, when 0, 20, 30, 40 or 50 µM of Ce4+ was dropped on it. Subsequently, the CDs + Ce4+ treated paper was immersed in various concentration of DA (0, 20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 µM). The FL recovery was recorded under 365 nm UV lamp.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to study the morphology and size of CDs. As presented in Fig. 1 a, CDs are torispherical spherical nanoparticles with the size of 4.95 ± 0.76 nm. To examine the chemical constituents and surface groups of the synthesized CDs, XPS and FT-IR spectra were performed. As shown in Fig. 1b, the peak at 3440 cm−1 belongs to the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The stretching vibration peaks of CH, CO, CC, CN, CC, CO locate at 2885, 1631, 1466, 1344, 1279/1242 and 1113 cm−1, respectively. The full-scan XPS (Fig. 1c), proved that CDs contained three elements, C, N and O, with the content of 71.19 %, 1.64 % and 27.17 %, respectively. The high-resolution (HR) C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) can be decomposed into three peaks at 283.3, 284.7 and 285.1 eV, which were designated as CC/CC, CN/CO and CO, respectively. In the HR N1s spectrum (Fig. 1e), the peaks at 398.9 and 399.4 eV were attributed to CN and NH. The HR O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) showed two distinct peaks centered at 531.2 and 531.4 eV, which were attributed to CO and CO/COC. Additionally, the crystallinity of the CDs was analyzed by XRD, revealing a distinct diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.1° corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite (Fig. S1), which confirms the presence of a graphitic structure in the CDs.

The optical properties of CDs was revealed by UV–vis absorption spectrum and FL spectra. Fig. 1g shows the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, the broad absorption band at 258 nm mainly corresponds to the π–π* transition of the CC/CC bonds, while the peak near 398 nm is attributed to the n–π* transition of the CN/CO bonds. The Fig. 1g illustration shows that the CDs solution appears colorless under daylight but exhibits orange FL under 365 nm UV light. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of CDs is 400 and 556 nm, respectively (Fig. 1h). The emission spectra of CDs was displayed in Fig. 1i, when the excitation wavelength changed from 380 to 420 nm, the emission peak of CDs remained at 556 nm. Subsequently, we investigated the photostability and ionic stability of CDs. As shown in Fig. S2a, the FL intensity of CDs did not change significantly after 7 h of UV irradiation, indicating that CDs exhibited strong light radiation resistance. In addition, when the concentration of NaCl reached 600 μM, the effect on the FL intensity is negligible (Fig. S2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs for detection of Ce4+ and DA
Fluorescence “ON-OFF-ON” strategy to detect Ce4+ and DA
ce:section.ce:para: Compared to other substances, Ce4+ can significantly quench the FL of CDs (Fig. 2 a). In addition, to optimize the analysis process, the effect of incubation time was studied. When (100 μM) of Ce4+ was added to CDs solution (200 μg/mL), the FL signal at 556 nm wore off in 10 min and then held steady (Fig. 2b). Therefore, 10 min of incubation is enough for the detection of Ce4+. On this basis, we decided to develop CDs as a sensor for the detection of Ce4+ via FL ON-OFF mode. We then quantitatively evaluated the ability of CDs to detect Ce4+. As shown in Fig. 2c, with the increase of Ce4+ concentration (0–100 µM), the FL intensity of CDs gradually decreased. The similar result was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S3). Fig. 2d–e presented the relationship between the concentration of Ce4+ and I0/I-1, where I0 and I is the FL intensity of CDs without and with Ce4+, respectively. In the range of 10 to 70 µM, I0/I-1 = 0.0499[Ce4+] − 0.5208, R2 = 0.9920, with a LOD of 0.14 µM (Fig. 2e). Compared to other methods (Table. S1), the CDs probe demonstrated greater sensitivity. To valuate the specificity of CDs on Ce4+, a total of 15 substances (Cr3+, CA, Glu, Nd3+, Pb2+, Urea, Ni2+, Co2+, L-Trp, Ce3+, NH4+, L-Leu, Y3+, Mn2+, MEL) were used as interferents. As depicted in Fig. 2f, these interferents did not affect the FL of the CDs + Ce4+ system, illustrating that CDs had significant selectivity for Ce4+.

It was worth noting that the FL intensity of CDs + Ce4+ was significantly recovered (∼90 %) after adding DA (Fig. 2g), demonstrating that CDs + Ce4+ can detect DA by FL OFF-ON pathway. And the FL recovery process stabilized within 25 min (Fig. 2h). As shown in Fig. 2i–g, the FL intensity of CDs slowly recovered with increasing DA concentration (0–70 µM). The same phenomenon was observed under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. S4). In the range of 20–50 µM, a linear equation was fitted: I/I0–1 = 0.0756 [DA] + 0.881, R2 = 0.9961, and the LOD was calculated to be 1.93 µM (Fig. 2k). Compared with the previous report, this CDs + Ce4+ sensory platform possessed higher sensitivity (Table. S2). The addition of various substances including Cr3+, Glu, Pro, NO3 −, SO4 2−, Ni2+, Urea, Y3+, Mn2+, L-Leu, Phe, Ser, L-Trp, Nd3+ could neither trigger any significant FL fluctuation nor affect the FL recovery of CDs (Fig. 2l), demonstrating that CDs + Ce4+ exhibited excellent selectivity for DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrophotometric detection of Ce4+ and DA
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, Ce4+, CDs + Ce4+ were presented in Fig. 3 a, where CDs + Ce4+ solution had two characteristic peaks at 256 and 400 nm (Fig. 3a). With the increase of Ce4+ concentration (0–60 µM), the absorbance at 256 nm was featured by a gradual increasing trend (Fig. 3b). There was a good linear relationship between (A0-A)/A and Ce4+ concentration (5–50 µM), where A0 and A was the absorbance at 256 nm without and with Ce4+. The linear equation was (A0-A)/A = −0.0051[Ce4+] + 0.2845, R2 = 0.9993, and LOD was 0.54 µM (Fig. 3c). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs + Ce4++DA, has a feature peak at 288 nm (Fig. 3d). With the increase of DA concentration (30–100 µM), the absorbance at 288 nm grew in strength (Fig. 3e). As shown in Fig. 3f, in the range of 45–100 µM, a linear equation was fitted: (A0-A)/A = 0.0033[DA] − 0.1006, R2 = 0.9906, and the LOD was 0.85 µM.
A0 was the absorbance at 256 nm of CDs. A was the absorbance at 288 nm of CDs + Ce4++DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanisms of detection Ce4+ and DA
ce:section.ce:para: The FL lifetime of CDs and CDs + Ce4+ was 15.62 and 8.73 ns, respectively (Fig. S5a), indicating the FL of CDs was quenched via a dynamic quenching process [47]. Since there was no significant overlap between the emission of CDs and UV–vis absorption of Ce4+ (Fig. S5b). The possibility of FL resonance energy transfer was ruled out. According to Lewis's acid–base theory, the NH and C=O groups present in CDs are Lewis bases, which Ce4+ is considered as a Lewis acid [48]. Therefore, Ce4+ is likely to coordinate with the NH and C=O groups on the surface of CDs, resulting in FL quenching. Furthermore, the presence of Ce4+ facilitates intramolecular electron transfer from the excited state of CDs to the unoccupied f-orbitals of Ce4+, resulting in FL quenching [49]. When DA was added to the CDs + Ce4+, Ce4+ preferentially coordinated with DA, causing Ce4+ to detach from the surface of CDs and the FL of CDs was restored. Thus, the FL lifetime of CDs recovered to 12.75 ns (Fig. S5a).

In the practical application of CDs, Ce4+ and DA not only has a crucial influence in the living body, but also exists in the environment and key industrial production that are closely related to people's lives. Therefore, it is necessary to evaluate the ability of the probe to detect Ce4+ and DA in real actual samples (tap water, drinking water and humane urine). The feasibility of the CDs sensor for detecting Ce4+ and DA in actual sample was evaluated by fluorometric mode. Ce4+ and DA were determined by standard addition methods. As shown in Table S3–S4, the recovery rate of real samples was 95.27 %–107.75 %. These results confirmed the feasibility of CDs as a sensor to detect Ce4+ and DA in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity and cell imaging of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to expand the biological application of CDs, there is an urgent need to assess the levels of Ce4+ and DA in vitro. Firstly, we evaluated the biocompatibility of CDs by MTT assay. The effect of CDs on L929 cells viability was studied. The results showed that the survival rate of L929 cells remained 80 % after co-incubation with CDs (125 μg/mL) for 24 h (Fig. 4 a), indicating that CDs had good biocompatibility.

Subsequently, the application prospect of CDs in cellular imaging was evaluated through a high content analysis system (HCA) system. As depicted in Fig. 4b, after co-incubation with CDs at 37 °C for 4 h, the cells emitted bright orange FL. However, the FL faded away when Ce4+ was added to the cells (Fig. 4c–e). After the addition of DA, the FL in the cells gradually recovered (Fig. 4f–g). The above experiments showed that CDs can successfully detect Ce4+ and DA in vitro through a fluorescent “ON-OFF-ON” process, illustrating the great potential of CDs in bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds-based paper sensor for detecting Ce4+ and DA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to easily and intuitively analyze Ce4+ and DA, we made CDs-based sensor. The filter paper was cut into circles with a diameter of 1 cm, the circles were soaked in CDs solution for 10 min, and dried at room temperature. Then, the CDs-based paper sensor was soaked in various concentration of Ce4+ solution (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 µM) and dried at room temperature for 30 min. The emission of CDs-based paper was monitored under 365 nm UV light. With increasing Ce4+ concentration, the FL changes from yellow to light purple (Fig. 4h). In the same way, CDs + Ce4+ paper sensor was treated with different concentration of DA (20, 30, 40, 50 and 60 µM), and the FL color gradually changed from light purple to yellow (Fig. 4i). In addition, we analyzed the RGB values of the paper with a color recognition app. As shown in Fig. S6a, there is a good linear relationship between R value and Ce4+ concentration. Fig. S6b indicated that there was a good linear relationship between R/G value and DA concentration. All these results indicated that CDs can be used as fluorescent probes to develop miniaturized analytical devices for the simultaneous detection of Ce4+ and DA.

To evaluate the practical applicability of the sensor, we tested its performance across different smartphone operating systems. As shown in Fig. S7 and Table S5, the CDs-based paper sensor exhibited consistent detection results for Ce4+ and DA, regardless of the smartphone system used (Apple Inc. or Android). The recovery rates for Ce4+ and DA were within a narrow range (94.03 % to 108.39 % for Ce4+ and 99.82 % to 108.39 % for DA), indicating that the sensor's performance is robust and reliable across different devices. These results demonstrate the potential of the CDs-based paper sensor for practical, on-site detection of Ce4+ and DA.

In summary, CDs were prepared by a simple solvothermal method, which could detect Ce4+ and DA via fluorometric and spectrophotometric bimodal. The CDs-based FL detection platform could analyze Ce4+ and DA in tap water, drinking water and DA in artificial urine with good recovery rate. Furthermore, CDs were successfully applied to sensing Ce4+ and DA in live cells. Benefiting from the distinct advantages of high sensitivity and fast response, CDs were used to construct paper sensor for the detection of Ce4+ and DA. This work opens up a promising prospect for the development of multifunctional sensors for environmental monitoring and bioanalysis.
Yifan Sun: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20250699KJ).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.113750.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorometric and colorimetric detection of cerium (IV) ion using carbon dots and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-ferrum (II) complex
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

Han, Fluorometric and colorimetric detection of cerium (IV) ion using carbon dots and bathophenanthroline-disulfonate-ferrum (II) complex, Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy, 264(2021) 120295, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120295.

J. Fluoresc.

Environ. Pollut.

J. Mater. Sci. Technol.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Structural and functional insights in polysaccharides coated cerium oxide nanoparticles and their potential biomedical applications: A review
